text: Thank you very much. I'm Vahag Nicolian from the Oregon Health and Science University, and I'm presenting on behalf of my team. This is a case of a robotic transabdominal preperitoneal inguinal hernia repair in a patient with an infrapubic penile prosthesis. Disclosures, Dr. Orenstein and myself serve as instructors and consultants for a variety of groups, though these are not relevant to today's conversation.

This was an eighty-eight-year-old man who presented with a history of atrial fibrillation and infrapubic penile prosthesis and a symptomatic recurrent right inguinal hernia. His hernia history was notable for an emergent right inguinal hernia repair with mesh for incarceration. The operative report described a blown-out floor and a high likelihood to recur. He had had an immediate recurrence within weeks of the operation and had multiple visits to the ED for pain and partial incarcerations.

When operating on patients with infrapubic penile implants, it's important to understand the components and the type of implant in place. This patient had a scrotal pump device with a reservoir placed in the space of retzius. In these particular cases, the tubing connecting the reservoir to the system can exit suprapubic or through the external ring. The term infrapubic penile implant is actually referring to the incision that was made in the skin when the implant was placed, not necessarily related to the tubing. It's a relative contraindication to MIS inguinal hernia repair, and we'll show why. So here's the CT scan. You find a retro rectus reservoir. You see inguinal scrotal hernia, and you saw some anterior mesh. Inguinal hernia repair for patients with infrapubic penile implants is usually done in the open approach. It's just safer.

However, this patient had had a recurrence, so we elected to move forward with MIS. When performing an MIS repair, it's important to be prepared for scarring that can occur with the reservoir to the bladder, the epigastrics, as well as the external iliac vessels, and have contingencies in place so that you can effectively manage these should they be injured. Mesh placement can be impacted because of the reservoir, so be aware that you may need to modify the mesh or remove the prosthesis at the time of the operation.

The surgeon begins with a peritoneal flap and proceeds with a lateral first dissection due to the presence of a reservoir. The dissection occurs in the pre-transversalis plane, transitioning to a pre-peritoneal dissection towards the indirect hernia sac. The moderate-sized inguinal scrotal hernia is reduced, revealing the epigastric vessels medially. A larger lateral pocket is developed to accommodate an extra-large mesh, while the medial dissection reveals scarring around the reservoir, appearing to adhere to the transversalis fascia.

The surgeon carefully dissects the initial scarring layer, revealing the encapsulated plastic reservoir. As the capsule is preserved, a small amount of serous fluid is released upon entry. The dissection progresses to the retropubic area, focusing around the obturator and vas deferens, establishing a critical view of safety for the myopectenial orifice.

The surgeon reviews the anatomy, showing the midline reservoir tubing, Cooper's ligament, femoral space, indirect space, and surrounding vessels. The reservoir is positioned outside its initial capsule, tracked over the pubis. An extra-large heavyweight mesh with a two-centimeter slit is placed to accommodate contralateral coverage, ensuring appropriate coverage of the myopectineal orifice.
The surgeon secures the mesh to the scar tissue instead of Cooper's ligament. The peritoneal flap is closed in a standard fashion, with no major intraoperative issues observed.

The patient did very well. We observed them overnight just to make sure things were alright. The patient was just discharged, required no narcotics, resumed anticoagulation within ten days of the procedure, and in surveillance is doing well with no signs of recurrence. So in conclusion, Patients who have a penile prosthesis and an inguinal hernia can have a minimally invasive approach to repair. It's important to review the implant and plan for your dissection appropriately. As always, whenever performing a MIS, inguinal, important to not compromise the critical view of the myopectinal orifice. Thank you very much.
